source: Invest Like the Best — AI, semiconductors, and the robotic frontier (EP.385)
summary: Growth equity and Series C investing is evolving toward a private equity operational value-add model as information advantages disappear
summary_extended: Baker argues that growth equity and Series C investing is undergoing a structural transformation toward a private equity-style operational value-add model, driven by the erosion of traditional information and sourcing advantages. He contends that the rise of crossover funds and mega-funds has effectively eliminated pricing and deal-access edges above the Series A/B stage, and that LLMs will further compress knowledge advantages that previously differentiated sophisticated investors. As a result, Baker believes the primary alpha source in late-stage venture will shift to genuine operational value-add — helping portfolio companies on talent, go-to-market, and execution — citing Valor as an example of a firm doing this authentically rather than as LP window-dressing. At the earliest stages, Baker sees judgment quotient (JQ) and team assessment remaining as the key differentiation, but the middle and growth stages will increasingly resemble private equity in their value-creation model. The implication is that generalist growth funds without true operational capabilities will face structural margin compression in returns.
detail: Baker argues that above Series A/B, sourcing and pricing advantages have been eliminated by the rise of crossover funds and mega-funds. LLMs will further democratize knowledge advantages. The differentiation will shift to genuine operational value-add (citing Valor as an example doing this authentically), not LP window-dressing support teams. Judgment quotient (JQ) and team assessment will be the primary alpha source at early stages.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a structural observation about the venture industry evolution; cites specific examples
quote_fragment: series C and growth equity is heading the way of private equity
